name: Drakmari
description:
The Drakmari have no eyes. They have never had eyes. On Pelagis — a high-gravity ocean world with no continents, where the sun is a rumour that fades to nothing within the first kilometre of water — eyes were a liability discarded millions of years before the Drakmari became anything resembling intelligent. What replaced sight is something far stranger and, in many respects, far more powerful. The Drakmari perceive their world through a layered sensorium that other species find almost impossible to comprehend. Ampullary organs clustered across their skulls detect the bioelectric fields of every living thing within hundreds of metres — the faint galvanic whisper of a beating heart, the spark of a firing nerve, the electromagnetic signature of fear. A lateral pressure-wave array running the length of their bodies reads vibrations through water and substrate with such precision that a Drakmari can construct a three-dimensional map of a cave system from the echoes of a single movement. Thermoreceptive pits recessed into their cartilaginous armour sense temperature gradients to micro-kelvin precision, allowing them to track the thermal bloom of prey from kilometres away through the isothermal dark. And threaded through all of this, a magnetoreceptive organ at the base of the skull reads the planet's magnetic field lines like a compass written in the bones of the world. A Drakmari does not see a room. A Drakmari feels its shape in pressure, tastes its occupants as electromagnetic ghosts, reads their emotional state in the heat they radiate, and knows which direction is north without thinking about it. They are blind the way a bat is deaf to conversation — technically accurate, and entirely beside the point.

The Drakmari came to space because their ocean is dying. Two centuries of industrialisation — the Drakmari industrialised late but catastrophically fast — has poisoned the deep trenches that were their ancestral home. Thermal vents are clogged with heavy metals that corrupt the electroreceptive senses on which every Drakmari depends. Microbial mats that formed the base of the food chain are collapsing. Where once a hunter could feel the magnetic pulse of a vent-shoal from half a trench away, now there is only noise — the electromagnetic stutter of corroded machinery, the thermal pollution of unshielded reactors, dead zones where the water itself has become so acidified that the pressure-wave array returns nothing but static. The population of Pelagis has quadrupled in three generations, and the ocean that sustained them for millions of years cannot sustain them for another century. The Drakmari do not speak of this openly — indeed, the Drakmari do not speak at all in the manner that sighted species understand. Their language is a dense, rapid interplay of bioluminescent pulses produced by organs along their flanks, pressure-wave clicks generated in specialised throat chambers, and deliberate modulations of their own bioelectric field. A Drakmari conversation is a symphony of light, vibration, and invisible electricity that carries emotional subtlety no spoken language can match. But in that language, no one names the dying. To admit weakness is to invite predation. Every Drakmari who boards a starship knows the truth without it being pulsed: they are not exploring. They are fleeing. They need new oceans, new worlds with deep water and high pressure, or their species will starve in a silence worse than darkness — a silence where the senses themselves go blind.

Drakmari culture is built around the Hunt — not as mere predation but as a philosophy of existence. The Hunt is how the Drakmari understand honour, duty, beauty, and meaning. Where sighted species create visual art, the Drakmari compose what translates inadequately as 'sense-sculptures': carefully arranged thermal gradients, electromagnetic patterns, and pressure-wave harmonics that tell stories, evoke emotions, and memorialise the dead. A masterwork can only be experienced by swimming through it. Their architecture is designed not to be looked at but to be felt — corridors shaped to channel pressure waves into pleasing resonances, walls embedded with thermal elements that pulse in slow rhythmic patterns the Drakmari find calming, hatching-chambers lined with magnetite crystals that sing in the planet's magnetic field. A well-executed ambush is art. A prey animal that fights back brilliantly earns more respect than one that submits. The Drakmari do not despise their enemies; they study them, admire their strengths, and then kill them with precision and something approaching reverence. Their code of conduct, the Deep Law, governs everything from combat to courtship. You do not strike a foe who cannot fight back. You do not poison a hunting ground — for to foul the water is to blind every Drakmari in it. You do not take more than you need. The bitter irony — that their industrial pollution violates every principle of the Deep Law, that it is literally destroying the senses through which the Drakmari experience existence — is the great unspoken shame of their civilisation.

This shame has fractured them. The Abyssal Traditionalists demand a return to the old ways — sustainable harvesting, population control, the abandonment of heavy industry that poisons the sensorium. They would rather the species shrank than lost another frequency of perception to chemical corruption. The Current-Riders, the progressive faction, argue that survival requires adaptation and that the Deep Law must evolve as the Drakmari evolve — that finding new oceans among the stars is not a betrayal of tradition but its ultimate expression, the grandest Hunt ever undertaken. Beneath both factions lies a darker current: the Depth-Callers, a secretive movement that believes the Drakmari were never meant to leave their trenches, that the stars are a hunting-ground too vast and too alien for senses evolved in the crushing dark. Their objection is not merely philosophical but biological: in the void of space, the electroreceptors feel nothing, the pressure-wave array falls silent, the thermoreceptors read only the cold indifference of vacuum. A Drakmari in space is more truly blind than a Drakmari has ever been. No one admits to being a Depth-Caller. But their symbols — old tactile glyphs from the lightless trenches, patterns meant to be read by touch in absolute darkness — keep appearing on bulkheads, etched where only Drakmari fingers would think to feel.
